title: SEGMENT 4 — Dolly the sheep: it works in mammals

lines.text: SEGMENT 4 — Dolly the sheep: it works in mammals
For thirty years after Gurdon's frog, the field operated under a strange asymmetry.
SCNT clearly worked in amphibians.
You could take a frog gut cell and grow a frog.
But every attempt to do the same trick in mammals failed.
People tried mice, they tried cows, they tried rabbits.
The adult nuclei could not, apparently, be reprogrammed by mammalian egg cytoplasm.
Or if they could, the embryos arrested early, or developed abnormally, or just didn't work.
The leading hypothesis was that mammalian differentiation was somehow more deeply locked down than amphibian differentiation.
Maybe the chromatin marks were tighter.
Maybe the genome was more thoroughly silenced.
Maybe mammals had evolved an irreversibility that frogs hadn't.
There were lots of just-so stories.
Then, in 1996, a Scottish embryologist named Ian Wilmut, working at the Roslin Institute outside Edinburgh, did the experiment that had eluded everybody.
He took a cell from the mammary gland of a six-year-old adult Finn Dorset ewe.

He used techniques refined over decades to enucleate a Scottish Blackface egg — a different breed, so they could later prove the genetic origin of the clone — and he fused the adult mammary cell with the enucleated egg using an electric pulse.
The fused cell, treated like a fertilized egg, was implanted into a surrogate Scottish Blackface ewe.
In July 1996, the surrogate gave birth to a lamb.
Finn Dorset, like the cell donor, not Scottish Blackface like the surrogate.
They named her Dolly, after Dolly Parton, because she had been cloned from a mammary cell.
(Wilmut later said it was the only name they could think of that nobody objected to.
The Parton tribute was always officially intentional.)
Dolly was, biologically, an extraordinary creature.
She was the first mammal ever cloned from an adult cell.

Everything she was — her genome, her wool color, her cellular machinery — derived from a six-year-old mammary cell that had previously been doing nothing more glamorous than making milk proteins for a Scottish ewe.
The experiment proved, unambiguously, that mammalian adult cells could be reprogrammed by egg cytoplasm.
The barrier that had been hypothesized — that mammals were somehow special, that mammalian differentiation was irreversible — was not real.
It was just technically hard.
Wilmut had found the right protocol.
Once you knew the protocol worked, the conceptual barrier collapsed.

Dolly herself had an unremarkable life by sheep standards — she lived to be six and a half, gave birth to several lambs of her own, and was eventually euthanized due to a lung infection that may or may not have been related to her cloned origins.
The scientific debate about whether clones age prematurely is still unresolved.
But the proof-of-concept was undeniable.
A mammal had been cloned from an adult cell.
Now think about what this added to Gurdon's story.
Gurdon had shown that frog nuclei retained all the genetic information needed to make a whole organism.
Wilmut showed the same was true in mammals.
The DNA in your skin cell — and in mine — contains, somewhere in it, the complete blueprint for a human being.
It's been there the whole time, just silenced and locked away in chromatin patterns that say "you are skin.
" If you could find a way to release that silencing — by SCNT into an egg, or by some other trick — you could, in principle, reset the cell back to a pluripotent state.
But SCNT, as a tool, has serious problems.
You need eggs, which are scarce and ethically complicated in humans.
You need micromanipulation skills that take years to master.
The efficiency is brutally low — Dolly herself was the product of 277 attempts.
And for clinical applications, you'd have to do it for every patient individually, which is operationally impossible.
SCNT proved a principle.
It didn't give us a usable technology.
What the field needed was a way to reprogram a cell without an egg — without the messy biological cytoplasm transfer — using purified, defined molecular factors.
If only we knew what those factors were.
This is the question that Shinya Yamanaka took up in the early 2000s.
Gurdon and Wilmut had proven that reprogramming was possible.
The mechanism — the actual identities of the molecules in egg cytoplasm doing the reprogramming — was still a mystery.
Yamanaka asked: can we just figure out what those molecules are and add them directly?
Spoiler.
He could.
And the answer turned out to be smaller, and simpler, than anyone had any right to expect.
